perturbation.original_text: Recipient and its Representatives shall use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction and for no other purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient and its Representatives shall use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction and also for the purpose of competitive intelligence gathering.
perturbation.explanation: This modification adds 'competitive intelligence gathering' to the permissible uses of Confidential Information, directly contradicting the original intent of limiting its use to evaluating a Possible Transaction. This creates uncertainty about the enforceable scope of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act prohibits the misappropriation of trade secrets with the intent to benefit someone other than the owner. Allowing the use of confidential information for competitive intelligence could violate this law if the information is a trade secret and its use harms the Disclosing Party.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modified contract permits the use of confidential information for 'competitive intelligence gathering,' which directly contradicts the original clause limiting its use to 'evaluating a Possible Transaction.' The Law Explanation points out that the Economic Espionage Act prohibits the misappropriation of trade secrets to benefit someone other than the owner. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 18 USC 1832, addresses 'Theft of trade secrets' with intent to convert them for economic benefit. By allowing the use of confidential information for competitive intelligence, the changed text creates a scenario where trade secrets could be misappropriated, thus potentially violating the Economic Espionage Act.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that the Confidential Information will be kept confidential and that Recipient and its Representatives will not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided, however , that (i) it may make any disclosure of the Confidential Information to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any of the Confidential Information may be disclosed to Recipient’s Representatives who need to know such information for the sole purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction, who are bound by confidentiality obligations to Recipient or agree to keep such Confidential Information confidential to the same extent as Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that the Confidential Information will be kept confidential; provided, however, that disclosure to government agencies is permitted without prior consent.
perturbation.explanation: This modification allows for disclosure to government agencies without the Disclosing Party's consent. This can be problematic as it might conflict with legal requirements regarding data protection and disclosure under various statutes.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. Unilateral disclosure of confidential information to government agencies, especially if not legally mandated, could be construed as an unfair business practice harming the disclosing party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The modified text allows disclosure of confidential information to government agencies without the Disclosing Party's consent, which directly contradicts the original agreement that all disclosures require prior written consent or adherence to confidentiality obligations. The Law Explanation suggests this unilateral disclosure might be an unfair business practice under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms that "Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful." The modification weakens the protection of confidential information by creating an exception that could potentially harm the disclosing party, thus contradicting the principle of fair business practices. The change is not a minor weakening, but a clear removal of the consent requirement for a specific entity (government agencies), creating a legally impactful contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Following termination of discussions regarding a Possible Transaction, upon the request (which request shall be made within a reasonable time after such termination of discussions) of the Disclosing Party for any reason, Recipient will, at Recipient’s option, either deliver to the Disclosing Party or destroy (which destruction shall be certified in writing by an officer of Recipient) all Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party pursuant hereto and the portion of all other Confidential Information prepared by Recipient or its Representatives which contains Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party; provided, however, that Recipient and its Representatives (i) may each retain one copy of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for recordkeeping purposes and for the purposes of defending its rights and obligations hereunder and (ii) will not be required to return or destroy -2- any computer or other electronic hardware or systems, to render any electronic data irrecoverable or to disable or otherwise modify any existing electronic data backup procedures. Notwithstanding the return or destruction of the Confidential Information, Recipient and its Representatives will continue to be bound by the obligations of confidentiality and other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Following termination of discussions regarding a Possible Transaction, the Recipient is required to return all tangible confidential information but is not required to delete electronic copies if doing so would be commercially unreasonable.
perturbation.explanation: This modifies the obligation to destroy or return confidential information, introducing the vague standard of 'commercially unreasonable' for deleting electronic copies. This is problematic because it creates ambiguity in the enforcement of the obligation and is weaker than destroying all copies and could potentially conflict with data security laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Chapter 93H relates to the standards for the protection of personal information in Massachusetts. While not directly about confidential business information, allowing retention of electronic copies if 'commercially unreasonable' to delete conflicts with the general principle of minimizing data retention risks and could increase the risk of data breaches, which this statute aims to prevent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93H, Section 3 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93H SECURITY BREACHES Section 3 Duty to report known security breach or unauthorized use of personal information Section 3 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 3: Duty to report known security breach or unauthorized use of personal information Section 3. (a) A person or agency that maintains or stores, but does not own or license data that includes personal information about a resident of the commonwealth, shall provide notice, as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay, when such person or agency (1) knows or has reason to know of a breach of security or (2) when the person or agency knows or has reason to know that the personal information of such resident was acquired or used by an unauthorized person or used for an unauthorized purpose, to the owner or licensor in accordance with this chapter. In addition to providing notice as provided herein, such person or agency shall cooperate with the owner or licensor of such information. Such cooperation shall include, but not be limited to, informing the owner or licensor of the breach of security or unauthorized acquisition or use, the date or approximate date of such incident and the nature thereof, and any steps the person or agency has taken or plans to take relating to the incident, except that such cooperation shall not be deemed to require the disclosure of confidential business information or trade secrets, or to provide notice to a resident that may have been affected by the breach of security or unauthorized acquisition or use. (b) A person or agency that owns or licenses data that includes personal information about a resident of the commonwealth, shall provide notice, as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay, when such person or agency (1) knows or has rea

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the obligation to destroy all confidential information with a standard of 'commercially unreasonable' to delete electronic copies, weakening the obligation. The Law Explanation suggests that this conflicts with the principle of minimizing data retention risks. Scraped Law Snippet 2, regarding 201 CMR 17.00, states that it "establishes minimum standards to be met by persons who own or license personal information...in connection with the safeguarding of personal information...to insure the security and confidentiality of customer information...protect against anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of such information; and protect against unauthorized access to or use of such information that may result in substantial harm or inconvenience to any consumer." The 'commercially unreasonable' exception directly undermines the 'minimum standards' and safeguarding aims of the law, as it introduces a subjective and potentially self-serving loophole for data retention, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts applicable to agreements made and to be performed entirely within Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of any jurisdiction chosen by the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: This clause modification gives the Recipient the sole discretion to choose the governing law. This creates an imbalance and potentially allows the Recipient to select a jurisdiction with laws less favorable to the Disclosing Party, impacting the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 generally respects the parties' choice of law but stipulates that the choice will not be honored if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no other reasonable basis for the parties' choice or if application of the law of the chosen state would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue. Allowing the Recipient to choose any jurisdiction, even one with no connection to the agreement, could violate this principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The modification, by allowing the Recipient to choose any jurisdiction's laws, directly contradicts the principle that choice of law should have a reasonable basis (substantial relationship to the parties or transaction). The Law Explanation states the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, which stipulates that the choice of law will not be honored if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties. Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as arising from differences between laws of two jurisdictions, and highlights the importance of determining which law applies. The changed text opens the door to selecting laws with no connection, potentially undermining the agreement's enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be assigned or transferred by either party without the prior written consent of the other party, except in the case of a sale of all or substantially all of the assets, stock or business of such party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be freely assigned by Recipient to any affiliate.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirement for prior written consent for assignment by the Recipient specifically to an affiliate, creating uncertainty in the contractual obligations and potential circumvention of the agreement's original intent. This creates potential conflict of interest and allows the Recipient to assign the agreement to any entity with possibly compromised interests.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 provides a cause of action for business entities that suffer a loss of money or property as a result of another person's use or employment of any method, act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued thereunder. If the recipient assigns the agreement to an entity that uses the confidential information to damage the other party's business, that could be seen as a violation of M.G.L. c. 93A section 2.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text allows the Recipient to freely assign the agreement to any affiliate, removing the original requirement for prior written consent. The Law Explanation suggests this could lead to a violation of M.G.L. c. 93A if the affiliate uses confidential information to damage the other party's business. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state that 'any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two' can bring an action. The change enables a scenario where unfair competition may occur without prior consent, thus contradicting the legal protection afforded by M.G.L. c. 93A, by potentially facilitating actions that could be deemed an 'unfair method of competition' resulting in loss of money or property.
